protocol: environmental - heat exposure/heat exhaustion environmental - heat stroke (protocol 5 - 2)

dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for breathing problems at a church in the food pantry.
Hi, I'm Cliff and this is Georgie. We're emergency medical technicians with the Richmond squad. What made you call 911 today?
Uh, I felt real nauseous and had to vomit. I've had a really bad headache and chest pain.
Mr. Smith, can you wake up for us? Mr. Smith?
Yes, I'm here. I'm feeling really bad. Is everything okay?
Mr. Smith, what is your name?
Joe Smith.
Mr. Smith, can you tell me where you are?
I'm in the back of the church food pantry.
Mr. Smith, please squeeze my fingers for me.
(squeezes)
Mr. Smith, are you having trouble breathing or coughing?
No, nothing in my airway.
Partner, take vitals on Mr. Smith, starting with respirations. Mr. Smith, are you okay if we check your breathing now?
Resp is 57.
Mr. Smith, can you take a deep breath for me?
(deep breath)
Partner, let me know if you hear any strange sounds when I listen to Mr. Smith's lungs.
SpO₂ is 96%.
Partner, give Mr. Smith's pulse now. Mr. Smith, can you tell me how you feel in your chest and body?
Pulse is 107.
Mr. Smith, is your skin normal in color or is it paler or blue in any areas? What about your hands and feet?
A little sweaty, but the rest of me is okay.
BP is 76 over 51.
Blood glucose is 156.
EKG reading completed. It shows sinus tachycardia.
Mr. Smith, on a scale from 0 to 10, where 10 is the worst pain you've ever felt, how bad would you say your chest pain is now?
About an 8, maybe a little less when I sit still.
Does the chest pain come and go? Any movement or activity make it worse?
It comes and goes while I tried to walk over here before the EMTs arrived.
Mr. Smith, does the pain go into your neck or jaw?
No, it stays in my chest.
Mr. Smith, can you tell me everything else you're experiencing right now? Headache is new or had it for a while?
The headache came after I started feeling sick. And the chest pain is stronger now than before.
Mr. Smith, have you had any heart disease or high blood pressure in your medical history?
Yeah, I've had primary hypertension. I've been okay with the medication, I think.
Which medications are you currently taking for your medical conditions?
I take lisinopril and Microzide, they help me feel better normally.
Are you allergic to any medications or food?
No allergies and no problems from the medications.
Partner, are we seeing any irregularities in his chest or heart sounds?
Looks like maybe a right ventricular conduction delay, and there's a possible anterior infarct. Further EKG checks might be needed.
Partner, get the ECG monitor out and do a 4-lead and then 12-lead monitoring. That EKG reading better clarify things.
Done the 4-lead monitoring. Now working on triggering the 12-lead EKG now.
12-lead EKG is done and our interpretation is unusual for someone your age-RSR in V1/V2 with a small right bundle branch block pattern.
Going to do another 12-lead in just a second. No other complications detected yet.
Partner, do we have IV access and saline to give?
IV started in Mr. Smith's left forearm now. Administering 500 mL of normal saline. His condition appears improved now.
Partner, did the IV go through an extremity vein like in the EPCR?
Yes, in the left forearm. Vein accessed properly, no issues noted.
BP now is 183/115. That's a big jump. Follows up with the pulse which is 90. Seems elevated.
Respirations are 13 now.
SpO₂ is up to 98% this time.
Partner, have we confirmed the destination as VCU Health Systems or need another option?
Yes, we've confirmed with dispatch we'll transport Mr. Smith to VCU Health Systems.
Mr. Smith, do you prefer a specific hospital for your care, or is VCU Health Systems acceptable?
VCU Health Systems is fine, just please hurry.
Partner, assist me in getting Mr. Smith onto the stretcher. We need to make sure he's comfortable and vitals are stable.
Mr. Smith is lying still, ready for transport. IV secured and EKG monitoring running.
Mr. Smith, I see you're a bit diaphoretic-are you uncomfortable with that?
Yeah, I've been sweating a lot for a while now.
IV is in the left forearm. Let's go with transport.
Respirations are 17 now, trending down during transport.
Pulse now is 93. It's a bit elevated but stable for now.
Mr. Smith, your respirations dropped from 57 to 17. How do you feel now?
I feel a little better.
Our field impression remains cardiac concern. We'll keep monitoring and transport you to VCU Health Systems.
Transporting now, vitals stable, no adverse reactions noted.
SpO₂ is 97%.
We need to make sure IV is running and EKG is still stable before turning over. You good to go?
All systems stable and trending well. Things look good for turnover now.
Handing over to the hospital. Field impression is nausea, vomiting, and possible cardiac involvement. All monitoring and IV setup complete.
Vital signs trending upwards. 12-lead EKGs showed possible anterior infarct. IV fluids running and patient is GCS 15.
Final BP before handoff is 168/87. Pulse is 93, stable since last round.
IV is running fine. No complications from the IV or EKG since the last check.
Mr. Smith, any changes in the chest pain or severity since transport began?
Pain has gone down since the fluids. I feel a little better now.
Mr. Smith, have we missed anything important about your symptoms or medical history during our assessment?
Just the chest pain again-nothing more.
Just to confirm one more time, any new allergies or changes since we've been here?
No changes. Still no allergies.
Just need to confirm billing and medical history. Birthday and insurance information?
Consent and details obtained. Mr. Smith is on lisinopril and Microzide.
We followed protocol 5-2 for environmental heat exposure and heat stroke.
Patient is en route to VCU Health Systems and all interventions have been properly documented.
We first considered heat exposure and tachycardia but found no further evidence for that.
Final vitals recorded. Monitoring and IV complete for transfer to hospital team.
VCU Health Systems is the transport destination. Emergency transport in progress.
Mr. Smith condition appears stable for transport. No distress.
Pressing onward with transport for further medical evaluation.
Glucose is 0. That's a bookend we took for documentation.
Final EKG shows sinus rhythm by the end of transport-normalizes a bit after fluids.
Partner, wrap the IV lines and monitor. Ready for hospital sign off.
Completed! All patient vitals and setup ready for nurse.
Field impression: Nausea, vomiting, and possible cardiac involvement. All medications and interventions complete and well noted.
EKGs indicated anterior infarct; patient is stable. Consent documented and all vitals tracked as per guidelines.
VCU is safe for next-level care. No travel delays planned.
Movement smooth and secure. Patient is stable for handover.
VCU is correct destination for this report. All data is complete.
Case notes on file for ER; advised for them to review EKGs and consider anterior infarction.
